author: Mary Smith
tone: concerned, assertive, protective of retail investor interests
executive_summary: Mary Smith, an individual retail investor, strongly opposes the SEC's proposed rescission of Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) and Rule 610(e) of Regulation NMS. She argues these rules serve as essential backstops for best execution, promote market transparency by preventing locked and crossed markets, and sustain retail investor trust in U.S. markets. She urges the Commission to modernize rather than eliminate these protections.

positions.order_competition_rule: Not directly addressed, but implicitly supportive of rules that protect retail order execution quality and oppose removal of existing order protection mechanisms
positions.best_execution: Strongly supportive of maintaining Rule 611 as an objective backstop for best execution; concerned that rescission would leave retail investors reliant solely on broker-dealer internal routing with no regulatory floor
positions.access_fee_reform: Briefly mentioned as a potential area for modernization rather than outright rescission; suggests adjusting access fees as a preferable alternative to removing core protections
positions.transparency: Strongly supportive of maintaining transparency through prohibition of locked and crossed markets; opposes any changes that would increase market opacity or fragmentation across dark and lit venues

economic_arguments: Compliance costs and market fragmentation concerns cited by proponents of rescission are characterized as acceptable costs of maintaining a fair system

Rescission of Rule 611 could allow retail orders to be executed at inferior prices, effectively subsidizing institutional and high-frequency trader latency advantages at the expense of retail participants
Removal of objective price standards shifts execution quality responsibility entirely to broker-dealer internal routing, creating potential for conflicts of interest and inferior outcomes
liquidity_arguments: Fragmentation of markets into disparate dark and lit pools with varying price protections would reduce retail investors' ability to confidently assess market conditions
The NBBO serves as a primary and accessible indicator of market health for retail investors; its erosion would diminish market clarity
No explicit liquidity provision or market-making arguments made, but implicitly argues that retail participation underpins market health
competition_arguments: Rescission would tilt the playing field toward professional traders and high-frequency traders who can navigate a more complex, less regulated landscape
Retail investors lack the tools and sophistication to compete in a fragmented, unprotected market environment
Removing Rule 611 prioritizes institutional routing efficiency over retail price protection, creating an uneven competitive dynamic
retail_investor_arguments: Rule 611 provides a clear, objective, and trustworthy standard (NBBO) that retail investors rely upon when placing orders
Retail participation in U.S. markets is at an all-time high due to belief in market fairness and transparency; rescission threatens this confidence
Declining retail trust and participation could result if foundational protections are removed in favor of institutional efficiency
Individual investors are not equipped to independently verify or negotiate execution quality without a regulatory baseline
The Commission should modernize existing rules rather than eliminate protections that guarantee a baseline of fairness for all investors
recommended_actions: Reject the proposal to rescind Rule 611 and Rule 610(e) of Regulation NMS
If modernization is needed, improve the definition of protected quotes rather than eliminating the Order Protection Rule
Consider adjusting access fees as a targeted reform rather than removing core investor protections
Ensure any replacement framework is robust and proven before removing existing safeguards
key_quotes: I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Commission's proposal to rescind Rule 611 (the 'Order Protection Rule') and Rule 610(e) of Regulation NMS.

Without the objective 'backstop' of Rule 611, there is a significant risk that retail orders could be executed at inferior prices, essentially subsidizing institutional latency advantages at the expense of everyday participants.
If the market becomes a collection of disparate 'dark' and 'lit' pools with varying price protections, the ability for an individual to confidently enter the market will diminish.
Repealing these foundational rules signals that the Commission is prioritizing the efficiency of high-frequency trading and institutional routing over the price-protection of the individual.
These are costs of maintaining a fair system.

If the Commission believes the current rules are outdated, it should focus on modernizing them to reflect current market realities... rather than removing the protections that guarantee a baseline of fairness for all investors.
